trans-unit.source: Operational Maps Tutorials
trans-unit.target: Operational Maps Tutorials

trans-unit.source: Maps play a central role in humanitarian work, helping teams understand needs, capacities, and conditions on the ground. This page brings together practical, scenario-focused tutorials that show how to create clear and effective operational maps in QGIS. Each example highlights techniques you can adapt to your own data and context, supporting better coordination and decision-making in the field.
trans-unit.target: Maps play a central role in humanitarian work, helping teams understand needs, capacities, and conditions on the ground. This page brings together practical, scenario-focused tutorials that show how to create clear and effective operational maps in QGIS. Each example highlights techniques you can adapt to your own data and context, supporting better coordination and decision-making in the field.

trans-unit.source: Health Facility Capacity Map: Visualising with multi-variable point symbols
trans-unit.target: Health Facility Capacity Map: Visualising with multi-variable point symbols

trans-unit.source: A health facility capacity map is a practical and valuable tool for health preparedness and response. These maps help responders quickly identify the locations of health services, assess their capacity, and determine whether they are operational. In many situations, this information is crucial for making decisions about resource allocation, referral pathways, surge support, and identifying service gaps. The maps are typically based on datasets provided by governments or partner organizations. If official datasets are not available, OpenStreetMap can serve as a good starting point.
trans-unit.target: A health facility capacity map is a practical and valuable tool for health preparedness and response. These maps help responders quickly identify the locations of health services, assess their capacity, and determine whether they are operational. In many situations, this information is crucial for making decisions about resource allocation, referral pathways, surge support, and identifying service gaps. The maps are typically based on datasets provided by governments or partner organizations. If official datasets are not available, OpenStreetMap can serve as a good starting point.

trans-unit.source: Health capacity maps usually combine multiple attributes into a single symbol by utilizing size, color, and different shapes.
trans-unit.target: Health capacity maps usually combine multiple attributes into a single symbol by utilizing size, color, and different shapes.

source.ph: name: Building a Multi-Variable Hospital Capacity Map Step by Step

target.ph: name: Building a Multi-Variable Hospital Capacity Map Step by Step

trans-unit.source: Building a Multi-Variable Hospital Capacity Map Step by Step :::
trans-unit.target: Building a Multi-Variable Hospital Capacity Map Step by Step :::

trans-unit.source: In this tutorial, you will learn how to create a multi-variable point map of hospitals in Malawi using QGIS. You will work with a modified version of the Malawi Master Health Facility Registry (with fictitious figures and hospital bed counts added for training purposes) and apply a combination of proportional symbol size, manual classification, and data-defined color overrides to effectively communicate both capacity and operational status at a glance.
trans-unit.target: In this tutorial, you will learn how to create a multi-variable point map of hospitals in Malawi using QGIS. You will work with a modified version of the Malawi Master Health Facility Registry (with fictitious figures and hospital bed counts added for training purposes) and apply a combination of proportional symbol size, manual classification, and data-defined color overrides to effectively communicate both capacity and operational status at a glance.

trans-unit.source: About the Dataset
trans-unit.target: About the Dataset

trans-unit.source: The data used in this exercise comes from the Malawi Master Health Facility Registry (MHFR), the official national database of all health facilities in Malawi. **[Malawi - Health Facility Registry](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/malawi-health-facility-registry)** It exists to provide a single, up-to-date source of information for planning and monitoring health services.
trans-unit.target: The data used in this exercise comes from the Malawi Master Health Facility Registry (MHFR), the official national database of all health facilities in Malawi. **[Malawi - Health Facility Registry](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/malawi-health-facility-registry)** It exists to provide a single, up-to-date source of information for planning and monitoring health services.

trans-unit.source: ⚠️ **Note:** For the purpose of this tutorial, the dataset has been **manipulated**.
trans-unit.target: ⚠️ **Note:** For the purpose of this tutorial, the dataset has been **manipulated**.

trans-unit.source: Download the data folder [**here**](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/module_4/exercise_6/Module_4_Exercise_Malawi_Health_Facilities_Registry.zip) and save it on your PC. Unzip the .zip file.
trans-unit.target: Download the data folder [**here**](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/module_4/exercise_6/Module_4_Exercise_Malawi_Health_Facilities_Registry.zip) and save it on your PC. Unzip the .zip file.

trans-unit.source: Health Facilit Capacity Map Tutorial
trans-unit.target: Health Facilit Capacity Map Tutorial

trans-unit.source: Data preparation
trans-unit.target: Data preparation

trans-unit.source: First, we need to load the Malawi - Health Facility Registry dataset into QGIS
trans-unit.target: First, we need to load the Malawi - Health Facility Registry dataset into QGIS

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Import the Malawi health facilities CSV into QGIS.
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Import the Malawi health facilities CSV into QGIS.

trans-unit.source: Import the Malawi health facilities CSV into QGIS
trans-unit.target: Import the Malawi health facilities CSV into QGIS

trans-unit.source: In the top menu, go to **Layer → Add Layer → Add Delimited Text Layer…**
trans-unit.target: In the top menu, go to **Layer → Add Layer → Add Delimited Text Layer…**

trans-unit.source: Next to the **File name** field, click the three dots ![](/fig/Three_points.png) and browse to your **Malawi health facilities CSV** file and click `Open`.
trans-unit.target: Next to the **File name** field, click the three dots ![](/fig/Three_points.png) and browse to your **Malawi health facilities CSV** file and click `Open`.

trans-unit.source: After selecting the file, QGIS will show a preview of the table. Take a moment to review the columns:
trans-unit.target: After selecting the file, QGIS will show a preview of the table. Take a moment to review the columns:

trans-unit.source: `ZONE`, `DISTRICT`
trans-unit.target: `ZONE`, `DISTRICT`

trans-unit.source: `LATITUDE`, `LONGITUDE`
trans-unit.target: `LATITUDE`, `LONGITUDE`

trans-unit.source: `Number_Beds` These fields contain all information needed for the mapping exercise.
trans-unit.target: `Number_Beds` These fields contain all information needed for the mapping exercise.

trans-unit.source: Under **Geometry Definition**:
trans-unit.target: Under **Geometry Definition**:

trans-unit.source: Select **Point coordinates**.
trans-unit.target: Select **Point coordinates**.

trans-unit.source: Set **X field** = `LONGITUDE`.
trans-unit.target: Set **X field** = `LONGITUDE`.

trans-unit.source: Set **Y field** = `LATITUDE`.
trans-unit.target: Set **Y field** = `LATITUDE`.

trans-unit.source: Ensure the **Geometry CRS** is set to **EPSG:4326 – WGS 84**.
trans-unit.target: Ensure the **Geometry CRS** is set to **EPSG:4326 – WGS 84**.

trans-unit.source: Click **Add**. The layer will now appear in your **Layers** panel and the points will display on the map canvas.
trans-unit.target: Click **Add**. The layer will now appear in your **Layers** panel and the points will display on the map canvas.

trans-unit.source: Next, we need to reduce the points visualised on the map to the facilities that actually have hospital beds. In this tutorial, these are `Central Hospital`, `District Hospital`, and `Hospital`.
trans-unit.target: Next, we need to reduce the points visualised on the map to the facilities that actually have hospital beds. In this tutorial, these are `Central Hospital`, `District Hospital`, and `Hospital`.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Filter the dataset to see only hospitals (Video)
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Filter the dataset to see only hospitals (Video)

trans-unit.source: Filter the dataset to see only hospitals
trans-unit.target: Filter the dataset to see only hospitals

trans-unit.source: **Open the attribute table**
trans-unit.target: **Open the attribute table**

trans-unit.source: Right-click `Malawi_health_facilities_raw` → **Open Attribute Table**.
trans-unit.target: Right-click `Malawi_health_facilities_raw` → **Open Attribute Table**.

trans-unit.source: Look briefly at the key fields:
trans-unit.target: Look briefly at the key fields:

trans-unit.source: **TYPE** – identifies facility type. Hospitals usually appear as `Central Hospital`, `District Hospital`, or `Hospital`.
trans-unit.target: **TYPE** – identifies facility type. Hospitals usually appear as `Central Hospital`, `District Hospital`, or `Hospital`.

trans-unit.source: **STATUS** – should contain `Functional` or `Non-functional`.
trans-unit.target: **STATUS** – should contain `Functional` or `Non-functional`.

trans-unit.source: **Number_Beds** – mostly fill
trans-unit.target: **Number_Beds** – mostly fill

trans-unit.source: Filter the dataset to include only hospitals
trans-unit.target: Filter the dataset to include only hospitals

trans-unit.source: Right-click Malawi_health_facilities_raw → Filter…
trans-unit.target: Right-click Malawi_health_facilities_raw → Filter…

trans-unit.source: Enter the expression:
trans-unit.target: Enter the expression:

trans-unit.source: Click Test to check how many rows match, then OK.
trans-unit.target: Click Test to check how many rows match, then OK.

trans-unit.source: QGIS will now hide all non-hospital facilities.
trans-unit.target: QGIS will now hide all non-hospital facilities.

trans-unit.source: Your filtered layer now shows only hospital facilities.
trans-unit.target: Your filtered layer now shows only hospital facilities.

trans-unit.source: Visualising the number of beds with proportional circle methods
trans-unit.target: Visualising the number of beds with proportional circle methods

trans-unit.source: Now that your layer is filtered to show only hospitals, you can create a map that shows hospital capacity using proportional circles. The number of beds (`Number_Beds`) will control the **size** of each symbol.
trans-unit.target: Now that your layer is filtered to show only hospitals, you can create a map that shows hospital capacity using proportional circles. The number of beds (`Number_Beds`) will control the **size** of each symbol.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Visualise hospital capacity using proportional (graduated-size) circles (video)
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Visualise hospital capacity using proportional (graduated-size) circles (video)

trans-unit.source: Visualise hospital capacity using proportional circles (graduated-size)
trans-unit.target: Visualise hospital capacity using proportional circles (graduated-size)

trans-unit.source: **Open the Layer Styling panel**
trans-unit.target: **Open the Layer Styling panel**

trans-unit.source: Select your hospital layer (the filtered `Malawi_health_facilities_raw`).
trans-unit.target: Select your hospital layer (the filtered `Malawi_health_facilities_raw`).

trans-unit.source: Right-click the layer → **Properties…** → **Symbology**.
trans-unit.target: Right-click the layer → **Properties…** → **Symbology**.

trans-unit.source: **Change the renderer to Graduated**
trans-unit.target: **Change the renderer to Graduated**

trans-unit.source: At the top of the Symbology window, change the style from **Single Symbol** to **Graduated**.
trans-unit.target: At the top of the Symbology window, change the style from **Single Symbol** to **Graduated**.

trans-unit.source: **Select the attribute for symbol size**
trans-unit.target: **Select the attribute for symbol size**

trans-unit.source: Under **Value**, choose **`Number_Beds`**. This is the field that will control the size of each circle.
trans-unit.target: Under **Value**, choose **`Number_Beds`**. This is the field that will control the size of each circle.

trans-unit.source: **Change the Method to “Size”**
trans-unit.target: **Change the Method to “Size”**

trans-unit.source: Next to **Method**, change the default (usually “Color”) to **Size**. This turns the graduated classification into a **proportional circle map**.
trans-unit.target: Next to **Method**, change the default (usually “Color”) to **Size**. This turns the graduated classification into a **proportional circle map**.

trans-unit.source: **Generate the classes**
trans-unit.target: **Generate the classes**

trans-unit.source: Click **Classify**. QGIS will create size classes based on the range of bed numbers in your dataset.
trans-unit.target: Click **Classify**. QGIS will create size classes based on the range of bed numbers in your dataset.

trans-unit.source: Adjust the classes manually (recommended)
trans-unit.target: Adjust the classes manually (recommended)

trans-unit.source: The data range is **1–200 beds**, but only a few hospitals have more than **80 beds**. Most hospitals are small or medium-sized.
trans-unit.target: The data range is **1–200 beds**, but only a few hospitals have more than **80 beds**. Most hospitals are small or medium-sized.

trans-unit.source: Automatic classification would cluster most facilities into one or two classes. To avoid this, we create **balanced, domain-informed classes**:
trans-unit.target: Automatic classification would cluster most facilities into one or two classes. To avoid this, we create **balanced, domain-informed classes**:

trans-unit.source: Class
trans-unit.target: Class

trans-unit.source: Bed Range
trans-unit.target: Bed Range

trans-unit.source: Meaning
trans-unit.target: Meaning

trans-unit.source: Very small hospitals
trans-unit.target: Very small hospitals

trans-unit.source: Small hospitals
trans-unit.target: Small hospitals

trans-unit.source: Medium hospitals
trans-unit.target: Medium hospitals

trans-unit.source: Large hospitals
trans-unit.target: Large hospitals

trans-unit.source: Very large / referral hospitals
trans-unit.target: Very large / referral hospitals

trans-unit.source: Adjust the Lower/Upper values for each class accordingly.
trans-unit.target: Adjust the Lower/Upper values for each class accordingly.

trans-unit.source: :::{note} Why these ranges?
trans-unit.target: :::{note} Why these ranges?

trans-unit.source: Most hospitals fall in the **1–60** bed range → we break this into three meaningful groups.
trans-unit.target: Most hospitals fall in the **1–60** bed range → we break this into three meaningful groups.

trans-unit.source: Few hospitals exceed **80 beds**, so the top class isolates the rare high-capacity referral facilities.
trans-unit.target: Few hospitals exceed **80 beds**, so the top class isolates the rare high-capacity referral facilities.

trans-unit.source: This ensures **variation in symbol size** is visible and not compressed into one tiny class. (See [Graduated Classification](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_3/en_qgis_data_classification.html#graduated-classification)) :::
trans-unit.target: This ensures **variation in symbol size** is visible and not compressed into one tiny class. (See [Graduated Classification](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_3/en_qgis_data_classification.html#graduated-classification)) :::

trans-unit.source: The resulting map displays hospitals as circles of different sizes, each size representing one of the bed-capacity classes you defined earlier. This gives a quick visual impression of where smaller and larger hospitals are located. However, at this stage the map only shows capacity: it does not yet communicate whether a hospital is functional or non-functional.
trans-unit.target: The resulting map displays hospitals as circles of different sizes, each size representing one of the bed-capacity classes you defined earlier. This gives a quick visual impression of where smaller and larger hospitals are located. However, at this stage the map only shows capacity: it does not yet communicate whether a hospital is functional or non-functional.

trans-unit.target: ::::{grid-item} **Proportional circle map showing the number of beds** :::{figure} /fig/en_Malwai_Exampel_proportional_circel_result.png

trans-unit.source: Proportional circles: beds ::: ::::
trans-unit.target: Proportional circles: beds ::: ::::

trans-unit.source: ::::{grid-item} **Classes of proportional circle map**
trans-unit.target: ::::{grid-item} **Classes of proportional circle map**

trans-unit.source: Smaller circles correspond to hospitals with few beds, while larger circles indicate facilities with higher capacity. :::{figure} /fig/en_Malwai_Exampel_proportional_circel_result_legend.png
trans-unit.target: Smaller circles correspond to hospitals with few beds, while larger circles indicate facilities with higher capacity. :::{figure} /fig/en_Malwai_Exampel_proportional_circel_result_legend.png

trans-unit.source: Adding visualisation of operational status with colour
trans-unit.target: Adding visualisation of operational status with colour

trans-unit.source: To visualise the operational status of each hospital (operational or non-operational) alongside its bed capacity, we can use QGIS’s data-defined override functionality. A **data-defined override** allows you to control a symbol property—such as colour, size, rotation, or opacity—using an expression or an attribute value from the layer. This means QGIS adjusts the symbol automatically for each feature, based on the data rather than manual styling. Using this technique, we can assign a colour to each hospital according to its status while keeping the proportional circle sizes for bed capacity.
trans-unit.target: To visualise the operational status of each hospital (operational or non-operational) alongside its bed capacity, we can use QGIS’s data-defined override functionality. A **data-defined override** allows you to control a symbol property—such as colour, size, rotation, or opacity—using an expression or an attribute value from the layer. This means QGIS adjusts the symbol automatically for each feature, based on the data rather than manual styling. Using this technique, we can assign a colour to each hospital according to its status while keeping the proportional circle sizes for bed capacity.

trans-unit.source: First, we need to open the **data-defined override Expression Builder**.
trans-unit.target: First, we need to open the **data-defined override Expression Builder**.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Open data-defined override to adjust colour based on operational status (Video)
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Open data-defined override to adjust colour based on operational status (Video)

trans-unit.source: Open data-defined override to adjust colour based on operational status
trans-unit.target: Open data-defined override to adjust colour based on operational status

trans-unit.source: Select your hospital layer (the filtered `Malawi_health_facilities_raw`).
trans-unit.target: Select your hospital layer (the filtered `Malawi_health_facilities_raw`).

trans-unit.source: Right-click the layer → **Properties…** → **Symbology**.
trans-unit.target: Right-click the layer → **Properties…** → **Symbology**.

trans-unit.source: Nex to `Symbol` Tab click on the dropdown menue, then click at the top click on `Configure Symbol`.
trans-unit.target: Nex to `Symbol` Tab click on the dropdown menue, then click at the top click on `Configure Symbol`.

trans-unit.source:
In the new window click on `Simpel Marker` and then next to `fill colour`on the `data-defined override` symbol![](en_data_defined_overried_icon.png)
::::
trans-unit.target:
In the new window click on `Simpel Marker` and then next to `fill colour`on the `data-defined override` symbol![](en_data_defined_overried_icon.png)
::::

trans-unit.source: To make this work, we now need to tell QGIS which colour to use for each hospital. Data-defined overrides use the QGIS expression language, which lets you write short rules that are applied automatically to every feature in the layer. In this step, we will write a small expression that checks the value in the STATUS field and assigns the correct colour based on whether the hospital is functional or not.
trans-unit.target: To make this work, we now need to tell QGIS which colour to use for each hospital. Data-defined overrides use the QGIS expression language, which lets you write short rules that are applied automatically to every feature in the layer. In this step, we will write a small expression that checks the value in the STATUS field and assigns the correct colour based on whether the hospital is functional or not.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Use an Expression to Colour Hospitals by Status (Video)
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Use an Expression to Colour Hospitals by Status (Video)

trans-unit.source: Use an Expression to Colour Hospitals by Status
trans-unit.target: Use an Expression to Colour Hospitals by Status

trans-unit.source: This expression tells QGIS to automatically choose a colour for each hospital based on its `STATUS` attribute. Functional hospitals become green, non-functional ones become red, and any missing values receive a neutral grey. The `CASE` statement works like an “if–else” rule: QGIS checks the value in the `STATUS` field and assigns the corresponding RGB colour ([RGB Color Picker](https://share.google/mYczZipa9EqVWFvyD)), ensuring every point is styled consistently without manual editing. You can copy the complete code here:
trans-unit.target: This expression tells QGIS to automatically choose a colour for each hospital based on its `STATUS` attribute. Functional hospitals become green, non-functional ones become red, and any missing values receive a neutral grey. The `CASE` statement works like an “if–else” rule: QGIS checks the value in the `STATUS` field and assigns the corresponding RGB colour ([RGB Color Picker](https://share.google/mYczZipa9EqVWFvyD)), ensuring every point is styled consistently without manual editing. You can copy the complete code here:

trans-unit.source: Or write the code yourself using the help of the functionality of the expression builder:
trans-unit.target: Or write the code yourself using the help of the functionality of the expression builder:

trans-unit.source: Making the Legend Match Your Map
trans-unit.target: Making the Legend Match Your Map

trans-unit.source: ⚠️ **Note:** When you use a data-defined override to colour your symbols, QGIS does not automatically update the legend.
trans-unit.target: ⚠️ **Note:** When you use a data-defined override to colour your symbols, QGIS does not automatically update the legend.

trans-unit.source: To make sure your map readers understand the meaning of the colours, you need to customise the legend manually. Here are two practical ways to do this.
trans-unit.target: To make sure your map readers understand the meaning of the colours, you need to customise the legend manually. Here are two practical ways to do this.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Option 1: Legend with helper layer
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Option 1: Legend with helper layer

trans-unit.source: A straightforward workaround is to duplicate your hospital layer and use these copies solely as legend helpers. Rename the duplicate for easy identification and change its color in the visualization. Make sure to hide this layer in the map view.
trans-unit.target: A straightforward workaround is to duplicate your hospital layer and use these copies solely as legend helpers. Rename the duplicate for easy identification and change its color in the visualization. Make sure to hide this layer in the map view.

trans-unit.source: In the `Print layout`, you can add this layer to the legend. This way, the legend will display the correct colors, while your original layer, which contains the data-defined overrides, will still be responsible for the actual map styling.
trans-unit.target: In the `Print layout`, you can add this layer to the legend. This way, the legend will display the correct colors, while your original layer, which contains the data-defined overrides, will still be responsible for the actual map styling.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Option 2: Edit Legend Colours Directly in the Print Layout
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Option 2: Edit Legend Colours Directly in the Print Layout

trans-unit.source: Another option works only in the `Print Layout`. In the Legend, just add your hospital layer twice. The secound one, you can adjust the colour of each symbol directly in the Legend. Set each item to green. Using the option `Start a new column before this term`, you can place the two layers next to each other.
trans-unit.target: Another option works only in the `Print Layout`. In the Legend, just add your hospital layer twice. The secound one, you can adjust the colour of each symbol directly in the Legend. Set each item to green. Using the option `Start a new column before this term`, you can place the two layers next to each other.

trans-unit.source: Adding a Third Variable Using Stroke Style (Facility Type)
trans-unit.target: Adding a Third Variable Using Stroke Style (Facility Type)

trans-unit.source: Your hospital capacity map now contains a rich amount of information, and it is already useful in its current form. However, QGIS allows us to add an additional layer of meaning without creating new layers or changing the existing size and colour logic. One way to do this is by using the stroke—the outline of each symbol—which can be styled or patterned dynamically. In the next section, you will learn how to use the stroke style to represent a third attribute, making your visualisation even more informative while keeping it easy to read.
trans-unit.target: Your hospital capacity map now contains a rich amount of information, and it is already useful in its current form. However, QGIS allows us to add an additional layer of meaning without creating new layers or changing the existing size and colour logic. One way to do this is by using the stroke—the outline of each symbol—which can be styled or patterned dynamically. In the next section, you will learn how to use the stroke style to represent a third attribute, making your visualisation even more informative while keeping it easy to read.

trans-unit.source: Here is an example that distinguishes three hospital categories using clear and readable stroke patterns:
trans-unit.target: Here is an example that distinguishes three hospital categories using clear and readable stroke patterns:

trans-unit.source: **How this works:**
trans-unit.target: **How this works:**

trans-unit.source: `Central Hospital` → solid outline
trans-unit.target: `Central Hospital` → solid outline

trans-unit.source: `District Hospital` → dashed outline
trans-unit.target: `District Hospital` → dashed outline

trans-unit.source: `Hospital` → dotted outline
trans-unit.target: `Hospital` → dotted outline

trans-unit.source: `All others` → solid (fallback)

trans-unit.source: By adding stroke styles to your symbols, the map now carries a third layer of information while keeping the overall design compact and readable. This works best when you limit stroke styles to just a few meaningful categories; using too many patterns will quickly make the map visually noisy. Make sure your legend clearly explains what each pattern represents, and avoid combining stroke style with additional outline colours unless it is absolutely necessary—too many variations can overwhelm the reader. With these principles in mind, your updated map should now communicate three attributes at once in a clear and balanced way.
trans-unit.target: By adding stroke styles to your symbols, the map now carries a third layer of information while keeping the overall design compact and readable. This works best when you limit stroke styles to just a few meaningful categories; using too many patterns will quickly make the map visually noisy. Make sure your legend clearly explains what each pattern represents, and avoid combining stroke style with additional outline colours unless it is absolutely necessary—too many variations can overwhelm the reader. With these principles in mind, your updated map should now communicate three attributes at once in a clear and balanced way.

trans-unit.source: With three visual variables now shown in a single symbol, it’s important that the legend reflects all of them clearly. QGIS does not automatically update legend entries when data-defined overrides or stroke styles are used, so a few extra steps are needed to ensure the legend matches what appears on your map. In the next section, you will learn simple ways to adjust the legend so that all size classes, colours, and stroke styles are represented accurately.
trans-unit.target: With three visual variables now shown in a single symbol, it’s important that the legend reflects all of them clearly. QGIS does not automatically update legend entries when data-defined overrides or stroke styles are used, so a few extra steps are needed to ensure the legend matches what appears on your map. In the next section, you will learn simple ways to adjust the legend so that all size classes, colours, and stroke styles are represented accurately.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Legend with helper layer A straightforward workaround is to duplicate your hospital layer and use the copy solely as legend helpers. Rename the duplicated layer so it’s easy to recognise in the legend. Use 'categorised' classification. Adjust the stroke style (solid, dashed, dotted) to match the categories you used in your map. Make sure to hide the helper layer in the map view so it doesn’t appear on the actual map.
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Legend with helper layer A straightforward workaround is to duplicate your hospital layer and use the copy solely as legend helpers. Rename the duplicated layer so it’s easy to recognise in the legend. Use 'categorised' classification. Adjust the stroke style (solid, dashed, dotted) to match the categories you used in your map. Make sure to hide the helper layer in the map view so it doesn’t appear on the actual map.

trans-unit.source:
In the Print layout, add the helper layer to the legend. This ensures the legend shows the correct stroke patterns, while your original layer—with its data-defined overrides—continues to control the real map symbology.

trans-unit.source: 3W Map (Who, What, Where)
trans-unit.target: 3W Map (Who, What, Where)

trans-unit.source: __Introduction:__
trans-unit.target: __Introduction:__

trans-unit.source: A 3W info map is one of the most commonly requested products during the first month of an operation. __3W__ refers to __who, what, and where__, meaning which organizations and National Societies are involved in the disaster response, what activities they are carrying out, and where those activities are taking place. The 3W is typically updated regularly as additional actors join the operation and expand their sectoral work in affected locations. You’ll commonly find 3Ws included in SitReps and operational documents, shared on the GO Platform, and discussed during Joint Task Force meetings. In this section we will discuss the essential elements of a 3W map and how to create one using QGIS.
trans-unit.target: A 3W info map is one of the most commonly requested products during the first month of an operation. __3W__ refers to __who, what, and where__, meaning which organizations and National Societies are involved in the disaster response, what activities they are carrying out, and where those activities are taking place. The 3W is typically updated regularly as additional actors join the operation and expand their sectoral work in affected locations. You’ll commonly find 3Ws included in SitReps and operational documents, shared on the GO Platform, and discussed during Joint Task Force meetings. In this section we will discuss the essential elements of a 3W map and how to create one using QGIS.

trans-unit.source: 3W Map example: Turkish Red Crescent Response to Earthquake :::
trans-unit.target: 3W Map example: Turkish Red Crescent Response to Earthquake :::

trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Example: 3W Brazil Floods Distribution Map :::{figure} ../../fig/4.8_3W_Brazil_Floods.png

trans-unit.target: 3W Map example: Brazil Floods Distribution Map ::: ::::

trans-unit.source: __What is needed:__
trans-unit.target: __What is needed:__

trans-unit.source: A recent version of QGIS
trans-unit.target: A recent version of QGIS

trans-unit.source: Access to 3W data
trans-unit.target: Access to 3W data

trans-unit.source: [IFRC icons](https://learn-sims.org/style-guidance/logos-and-icons/) for map making
trans-unit.target: [IFRC icons](https://learn-sims.org/style-guidance/logos-and-icons/) for map making

trans-unit.source: Logos of the organizations involved
trans-unit.target: Logos of the organizations involved

trans-unit.source: Getting the 3W required data
trans-unit.target: Getting the 3W required data

trans-unit.source: A variety of factors can affect how much 3W information you have available. In the early weeks of a response, there may not yet be a structured process for gathering data from the different sectors and organizations involved. The IM Coordinator may recommend using a simple survey for sector leads and/or National Societies to capture an initial snapshot of who is doing what and where. As the operation becomes more organized, sector leads and National Societies can begin entering 3W data directly into a joined platform, which then can be used as the primary source for the 3W map.
trans-unit.target: A variety of factors can affect how much 3W information you have available. In the early weeks of a response, there may not yet be a structured process for gathering data from the different sectors and organizations involved. The IM Coordinator may recommend using a simple survey for sector leads and/or National Societies to capture an initial snapshot of who is doing what and where. As the operation becomes more organized, sector leads and National Societies can begin entering 3W data directly into a joined platform, which then can be used as the primary source for the 3W map.

trans-unit.source: If no formal 3W data collection system is in place, information might need to be extracted from SitReps and other reports produced by the operation team. Focus first on identifying the following:
trans-unit.target: If no formal 3W data collection system is in place, information might need to be extracted from SitReps and other reports produced by the operation team. Focus first on identifying the following:

trans-unit.source: __What response activities are the National Society involved in, and where?__
trans-unit.target: __What response activities are the National Society involved in, and where?__

trans-unit.source: __What response activities are being done by IFRC operation, and where?__
trans-unit.target: __What response activities are being done by IFRC operation, and where?__

trans-unit.source: __Key situation overview figures__, such as number of __people affected by the disaster__, __number of deaths or injuries__, number of houses damaged, number of geographical units affected (all of these data points can be modified based on the context of the disaster).
trans-unit.target: __Key situation overview figures__, such as number of __people affected by the disaster__, __number of deaths or injuries__, number of houses damaged, number of geographical units affected (all of these data points can be modified based on the context of the disaster).

trans-unit.source: Make sure to __note the data source__ you are using for key situation overview figures, and whenever possible to align the figures you are using to those used by the host National Society instead of using figures released by the media.
trans-unit.target: Make sure to __note the data source__ you are using for key situation overview figures, and whenever possible to align the figures you are using to those used by the host National Society instead of using figures released by the media.

trans-unit.source: How many people or households are being reached by sectoral activities, and where?
trans-unit.target: How many people or households are being reached by sectoral activities, and where?

trans-unit.source: It is normal that the initial dataset is limited. The 3W map will evolve and expand as additional information comes in.
trans-unit.target: It is normal that the initial dataset is limited. The 3W map will evolve and expand as additional information comes in.

trans-unit.source: :::{admonition} Helpful information on how to create such a 3W map can be found [here](https://learn-sims.org/information-design/creating-a-3w-who-what-where-infographic/).
trans-unit.target: :::{admonition} Helpful information on how to create such a 3W map can be found [here](https://learn-sims.org/information-design/creating-a-3w-who-what-where-infographic/).

trans-unit.source: For a variety of 3W map examples, browse the Maps/Infographics section on [Reliefweb](https://reliefweb.int/updates?view=maps&search=title%3A%223W%22). :::
trans-unit.target: For a variety of 3W map examples, browse the Maps/Infographics section on [Reliefweb](https://reliefweb.int/updates?view=maps&search=title%3A%223W%22). :::

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Example: Hurricane Melissa November 2025 | Jamaica The two 3W example maps below demonstrate how such __products develop__ as new information becomes available. The first map, produced at the beginning of November by a single organization (MapAction), contains limited data and represents an __initial community assessment__. Three weeks later, the second map created jointly by MapAction and OCHA, includes significantly more information. In addition to expanded data, the __map’s styling has also evolved__. This progression is entirely normal; maps are expected to __improve__ and become __more detailed__ over time. What matters most is establishing an initial version that can be updated and refined as the response advances.
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Example: Hurricane Melissa November 2025 | Jamaica The two 3W example maps below demonstrate how such __products develop__ as new information becomes available. The first map, produced at the beginning of November by a single organization (MapAction), contains limited data and represents an __initial community assessment__. Three weeks later, the second map created jointly by MapAction and OCHA, includes significantly more information. In addition to expanded data, the __map’s styling has also evolved__. This progression is entirely normal; maps are expected to __improve__ and become __more detailed__ over time. What matters most is establishing an initial version that can be updated and refined as the response advances.

trans-unit.source: Jamaica: Hurricane Melissa - Who, What, Where (3W) as of 03-Nov-2025: Community Assessments (Source: MapAction). :::
trans-unit.target: Jamaica: Hurricane Melissa - Who, What, Where (3W) as of 03-Nov-2025: Community Assessments (Source: MapAction). :::

trans-unit.target: Jamaica: Hurricane Melissa - Who, What, Where (3W) as of 24-Nov-2025 12:00L (Sources: MapAction, OCHA). :::

trans-unit.source: 3W Map Creation
trans-unit.target: 3W Map Creation

trans-unit.source: Add all the relevant spatial data. Used in probably every instance:
trans-unit.target: Add all the relevant spatial data. Used in probably every instance:

trans-unit.source: Administrative boundaries
trans-unit.target: Administrative boundaries

trans-unit.source: Affected areas with the relevant disaster
trans-unit.target: Affected areas with the relevant disaster

trans-unit.source: Points of Interest (Airports, Ports, Cities)
trans-unit.target: Points of Interest (Airports, Ports, Cities)

trans-unit.source: Background map (OpenStreetMap is always a solid choice)
trans-unit.target: Background map (OpenStreetMap is always a solid choice)

trans-unit.source: Import the 3W Activity data
trans-unit.target: Import the 3W Activity data

trans-unit.source: Add the 3W dataset to QGIS (mostly CSV or Excel)
trans-unit.target: Add the 3W dataset to QGIS (mostly CSV or Excel)

trans-unit.source: Ensure that it contains information about the organization, activity type, and location.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that it contains information about the organization, activity type, and location.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} How to import .csv or .txt
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} How to import .csv or .txt

trans-unit.source: __Delimited text import (.csv, .txt)__
trans-unit.target: __Delimited text import (.csv, .txt)__

trans-unit.source: When working with 3W data, the most common format encountered is a delimited text file, such as `.csv` files (Comma Separated Values). These files contain tabular data, which can be opened by programs such as Microsoft Excel. They can contain geographical or positional information as point coordinates in separated columns (for example, latitude and longitude, or x- and y-coordinates), or as "Well Known Text" (WKT), which represents complex geometries, such as polygons or lines. In some cases, the table may not include any geographic information at all. When this happens, the file is simply added as a non-spatial table, allowing access to its attribute information without displaying it on the map.
trans-unit.target: When working with 3W data, the most common format encountered is a delimited text file, such as `.csv` files (Comma Separated Values). These files contain tabular data, which can be opened by programs such as Microsoft Excel. They can contain geographical or positional information as point coordinates in separated columns (for example, latitude and longitude, or x- and y-coordinates), or as "Well Known Text" (WKT), which represents complex geometries, such as polygons or lines. In some cases, the table may not include any geographic information at all. When this happens, the file is simply added as a non-spatial table, allowing access to its attribute information without displaying it on the map.

trans-unit.source: __Open Delimited Text Layer__
trans-unit.target: __Open Delimited Text Layer__

trans-unit.source: Import delimited text in QGIS 3.36. :::
trans-unit.target: Import delimited text in QGIS 3.36. :::

trans-unit.source: `Layer` -> `Add Layer` -> `Open Delimited Text Layer`.
trans-unit.target: `Layer` -> `Add Layer` -> `Open Delimited Text Layer`.

trans-unit.source: Click on `File name` click on the three points ![](/fig/Three_points.png) and navigate to your CSV file and click `Open`.
trans-unit.target: Click on `File name` click on the three points ![](/fig/Three_points.png) and navigate to your CSV file and click `Open`.

trans-unit.source: `File Format`: Here you can specify which delimiter is used in the file you want to import. In a standard CSV file, commas `,` are used. If this is not the case, select `Custom delimiters`. Here you can choose the exact delimiter used in your file.
trans-unit.target: `File Format`: Here you can specify which delimiter is used in the file you want to import. In a standard CSV file, commas `,` are used. If this is not the case, select `Custom delimiters`. Here you can choose the exact delimiter used in your file.

trans-unit.source: Adjusting the file format parameters while importing a delimited text layer into QGIS. :::
trans-unit.target: Adjusting the file format parameters while importing a delimited text layer into QGIS. :::

trans-unit.source: If your CSV file contains geometry, continue with `Geometry definition`: In this section, you specify which columns of the file contain the spatial information to georeference the data on the map. If the file has a column containing __latitude__ and another with __longitude__ data, you can use them to georeferenced the data. Check `Point Coordinates` if the `.csv`-file contains point data. Select for `X field` “LONGITUDE” and for `Y field` “LATITUDE”.
trans-unit.target: If your CSV file contains geometry, continue with `Geometry definition`: In this section, you specify which columns of the file contain the spatial information to georeference the data on the map. If the file has a column containing __latitude__ and another with __longitude__ data, you can use them to georeferenced the data. Check `Point Coordinates` if the `.csv`-file contains point data. Select for `X field` “LONGITUDE” and for `Y field` “LATITUDE”.

trans-unit.source: Under `Geometry CRS` select the coordinate reference system (CRS). By default, QGIS will select the CRS of the project. If the file does not have spatial information choose the option `No geometry (attribute only table)`.
trans-unit.target: Under `Geometry CRS` select the coordinate reference system (CRS). By default, QGIS will select the CRS of the project. If the file does not have spatial information choose the option `No geometry (attribute only table)`.

trans-unit.source: Click `Add`
trans-unit.target: Click `Add`

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Video: Opening delimited text files in QGIS
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Video: Opening delimited text files in QGIS

trans-unit.source: Style the administrative boundaries with a transparent fill and choose an appropriate `Stroke color` and `Stroke width` to ensure they are clearly visible without obscuring underlying map content.
trans-unit.target: Style the administrative boundaries with a transparent fill and choose an appropriate `Stroke color` and `Stroke width` to ensure they are clearly visible without obscuring underlying map content.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Styling administrative boundaries
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Styling administrative boundaries

trans-unit.source: __Styling administrative boundaries (Polygons)__
trans-unit.target: __Styling administrative boundaries (Polygons)__

trans-unit.source: When creating 3W maps, administrative boundaries are always a key component. Most overview maps display disaster-related information aggregated by these boundaries. To visualize multiple administrative levels at once, each layer needs to be styled so that lower layers remain visible and the hierarchy between levels is clear. Often, this begins with adding the country outline and then progressively adding each subsequent administrative level, ensuring that the symbology distinguishes them without cluttering the map.
trans-unit.target: When creating 3W maps, administrative boundaries are always a key component. Most overview maps display disaster-related information aggregated by these boundaries. To visualize multiple administrative levels at once, each layer needs to be styled so that lower layers remain visible and the hierarchy between levels is clear. Often, this begins with adding the country outline and then progressively adding each subsequent administrative level, ensuring that the symbology distinguishes them without cluttering the map.

trans-unit.source: __Only display the outlines of polygons__
trans-unit.target: __Only display the outlines of polygons__

trans-unit.source: Now, we want to change the symbology of a layer so that __only the outlines of the polygons are visible__. This is necessary to make layers below this one visible.
trans-unit.target: Now, we want to change the symbology of a layer so that __only the outlines of the polygons are visible__. This is necessary to make layers below this one visible.

trans-unit.source: To change the symbology of a single layer:
trans-unit.target: To change the symbology of a single layer:

trans-unit.source: Open the `Styling panel` and navigate to the symbology tab. By default, the symbology will be set to `Single Symbol`. This means that the same colors and contours will be applied to all the features in that layer.
trans-unit.target: Open the `Styling panel` and navigate to the symbology tab. By default, the symbology will be set to `Single Symbol`. This means that the same colors and contours will be applied to all the features in that layer.

trans-unit.source: Click on `Simple Fill`.
trans-unit.target: Click on `Simple Fill`.

trans-unit.source: Click on the arrow to the right of `Fill Color`.
trans-unit.target: Click on the arrow to the right of `Fill Color`.

trans-unit.source: Check the `Transparent Fill` option.
trans-unit.target: Check the `Transparent Fill` option.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Video: Making the fill color transparent
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Video: Making the fill color transparent

trans-unit.source: __Adjusting the Styles of Multiple Overlaying Layers__
trans-unit.target: __Adjusting the Styles of Multiple Overlaying Layers__

trans-unit.source: __Ordering the layers__
trans-unit.target: __Ordering the layers__

trans-unit.source: Import all the administrative boundaries into your QGIS-project that you want to work with (e.g. Admin 0-2).
trans-unit.target: Import all the administrative boundaries into your QGIS-project that you want to work with (e.g. Admin 0-2).

trans-unit.source: One option is to order the layers in the Layers panel so that the `Admin0`-layer sits on top, followed by `Admin1` and `Admin2`. At first, this might look weird because `Admin0` will cover everything.
trans-unit.target: One option is to order the layers in the Layers panel so that the `Admin0`-layer sits on top, followed by `Admin1` and `Admin2`. At first, this might look weird because `Admin0` will cover everything.

trans-unit.source: Order the layers and navigate to the styling panel of the topmost layer :::
trans-unit.target: Order the layers and navigate to the styling panel of the topmost layer :::

trans-unit.source: Change the symbology of the `Admin0` layer by opening the styling panel and navigating to the Symbology tab.
trans-unit.target: Change the symbology of the `Admin0` layer by opening the styling panel and navigating to the Symbology tab.

trans-unit.source: Click on `Simple Fill` to open the style options.
trans-unit.target: Click on `Simple Fill` to open the style options.

trans-unit.source: Expand the `Fill Color` menu and check the `Transparent Fill` option. This will make only the boundaries visible, so __we will be able to see the layer under this one__.
trans-unit.target: Expand the `Fill Color` menu and check the `Transparent Fill` option. This will make only the boundaries visible, so __we will be able to see the layer under this one__.

trans-unit.source: Choose a `Stroke Color`, and adjust the `Stroke Width`.
trans-unit.target: Choose a `Stroke Color`, and adjust the `Stroke Width`.

trans-unit.source: Click OK.
trans-unit.target: Click OK.

trans-unit.source: __Repeat the same process__ for the `Admin1` layer, using the same color as for `Admin0` (it will be in "Recent colors). Adjust the stroke width so that it differs from `Admin0`, ensuring that the administrative levels remain visually distinct.
trans-unit.target: __Repeat the same process__ for the `Admin1` layer, using the same color as for `Admin0` (it will be in "Recent colors). Adjust the stroke width so that it differs from `Admin0`, ensuring that the administrative levels remain visually distinct.

trans-unit.source: Now we can see the boundaries of the country and its states, and behind that we can see the districts (`Admin2`).
trans-unit.target: Now we can see the boundaries of the country and its states, and behind that we can see the districts (`Admin2`).

trans-unit.source: Let's make the district layer's style consistent with the others.
trans-unit.target: Let's make the district layer's style consistent with the others.

trans-unit.source: Choose a `Fill Color`.
trans-unit.target: Choose a `Fill Color`.

trans-unit.source: Use the same `Stroke Color` as for `Admin0` and `Admin1`, but make the width even thiner and the Stroke Style a __Dash Line__.
trans-unit.target: Use the same `Stroke Color` as for `Admin0` and `Admin1`, but make the width even thiner and the Stroke Style a __Dash Line__.

trans-unit.source: Click OK and look at your map: hopefully it's starting to look nicer!
trans-unit.target: Click OK and look at your map: hopefully it's starting to look nicer!

trans-unit.source: The styling of a vector data consists of the color and the outline. :::
trans-unit.target: The styling of a vector data consists of the color and the outline. :::

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Video: Adjusting the style for multiple layers
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Video: Adjusting the style for multiple layers
:::

trans-unit.source: Style the disaster-related information by selecting a color ramp that fits the context. One common approach is to create a choropleth map using __Graduated__ styling, which visually represents variations in impact or case numbers across administrative areas.
trans-unit.target: Style the disaster-related information by selecting a color ramp that fits the context. One common approach is to create a choropleth map using __Graduated__ styling, which visually represents variations in impact or case numbers across administrative areas.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} Styling disaster-related information
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} Styling disaster-related information

trans-unit.source: __Creating a Choropleth Map ("Gradudated Styling)__
trans-unit.target: __Creating a Choropleth Map ("Gradudated Styling)__

trans-unit.source: If a layer contains numeric values that are continuous, they can be organized in intervals. These intervals can be displayed in graduated colors. In this example, colors are assigned to Admin1 polygons based on the total population of each State.
trans-unit.target: If a layer contains numeric values that are continuous, they can be organized in intervals. These intervals can be displayed in graduated colors. In this example, colors are assigned to Admin1 polygons based on the total population of each State.

trans-unit.source: Open the `Symbology` options and choose `Graduated`.
trans-unit.target: Open the `Symbology` options and choose `Graduated`.

trans-unit.source: __Select the value you want to use to assign colors__, in this case, it will be `total_pop`.
trans-unit.target: __Select the value you want to use to assign colors__, in this case, it will be `total_pop`.

trans-unit.source: With variable ranges, select __Graduated__ symbology and choose the attribute with continuous values :::
trans-unit.target: With variable ranges, select __Graduated__ symbology and choose the attribute with continuous values :::

trans-unit.source: Click on `Classify` to __list all values divided in classes__.
trans-unit.target: Click on `Classify` to __list all values divided in classes__.

trans-unit.source: Choose __how many classes__ you want the data to be divided into ‒ let's say 4. You can also assign different `Modes`. We usually work with `Equal Count` or `Equal Interval` depending on our data.
trans-unit.target: Choose __how many classes__ you want the data to be divided into ‒ let's say 4. You can also assign different `Modes`. We usually work with `Equal Count` or `Equal Interval` depending on our data.

trans-unit.source: By default, the color ramp will be red. However, red is not the right color to use for population count, as it is generally used to communicate negative elements, such as food insecurity or cholera cases.
trans-unit.target: By default, the color ramp will be red. However, red is not the right color to use for population count, as it is generally used to communicate negative elements, such as food insecurity or cholera cases.

trans-unit.source: Click on __the arrow next to the color ramp__ to choose another combination of colors - let's say a color ramp from white to blue.
trans-unit.target: Click on __the arrow next to the color ramp__ to choose another combination of colors - let's say a color ramp from white to blue.

trans-unit.source: Click `Apply` to preview the look of your layer, then `OK`.
trans-unit.target: Click `Apply` to preview the look of your layer, then `OK`.

trans-unit.source: You can categorize the continuous values into classes and assign a color ramp . :::
trans-unit.target: You can categorize the continuous values into classes and assign a color ramp . :::

trans-unit.source: __Single Labels__
trans-unit.target: __Single Labels__

trans-unit.source: Creates a single label style for every feature in the layer. You can select an attribute (value) which will be displayed. For example, the name of a district. You need to know which attribute displays the information you want to display. Look at the attribute table of the dataset to find it out.
trans-unit.target: Creates a single label style for every feature in the layer. You can select an attribute (value) which will be displayed. For example, the name of a district. You need to know which attribute displays the information you want to display. Look at the attribute table of the dataset to find it out.

trans-unit.source: Single labels for each administrative region (adm1) in Nigeria. The reader is able to assign each label to the respective administrative entity. :::
trans-unit.target: Single labels for each administrative region (adm1) in Nigeria. The reader is able to assign each label to the respective administrative entity. :::

trans-unit.source: Assigning the correct attribute value in the labeling options. QGIS needs to know which attribute (column) of the attribute table should be displayed as a label. In this case, we want the name of the administrative region (`ADM1_EN`) to be displayed. :::
trans-unit.target: Assigning the correct attribute value in the labeling options. QGIS needs to know which attribute (column) of the attribute table should be displayed as a label. In this case, we want the name of the administrative region (`ADM1_EN`) to be displayed. :::

trans-unit.source: __Adding Single Labels to a Layer__
trans-unit.target: __Adding Single Labels to a Layer__

trans-unit.source: In the styling panel, click on the `Labels`-tab underneath the Symbology tab.
trans-unit.target: In the styling panel, click on the `Labels`-tab underneath the Symbology tab.

trans-unit.source: Select `Single labels`.
trans-unit.target: Select `Single labels`.

trans-unit.source: `Value` is where you choose the attribute that will be displayed as a label. For example `ADM1_EN` will display the English names of Nigerian states for each feature in the data set.
trans-unit.target: `Value` is where you choose the attribute that will be displayed as a label. For example `ADM1_EN` will display the English names of Nigerian states for each feature in the data set.

trans-unit.source: Let's __change the font__: Open the font dropdown menu and select Arial. Make the text `Bold` in the Style dropdown menu. Change the color by clicking on `Color`, and change the `Size` to 8 pt.
trans-unit.target: Let's __change the font__: Open the font dropdown menu and select Arial. Make the text `Bold` in the Style dropdown menu. Change the color by clicking on `Color`, and change the `Size` to 8 pt.

trans-unit.source: Let's __add a white buffer__ around the label. In the `Labels` tab, you will find a list with different options to style the labels. Right now, we are in the `Text` menu. Select `Buffer` and check the `Draw text buffer` option. This will make the labels stand out more on dark or crowded maps.
trans-unit.target: Let's __add a white buffer__ around the label. In the `Labels` tab, you will find a list with different options to style the labels. Right now, we are in the `Text` menu. Select `Buffer` and check the `Draw text buffer` option. This will make the labels stand out more on dark or crowded maps.

trans-unit.source: Click `Apply` and `OK`.
trans-unit.target: Click `Apply` and `OK`.

trans-unit.source: Single Labels are not always useful. For example, if the dataset is too big, or you only want to display certain features in the dataset. In the example below, there are too many settlements to display labels for each settlement. Instead, it might be useful to only display the regional and national capitals. For such a use case, Rule-based Labeling is ideal.
trans-unit.target: Single Labels are not always useful. For example, if the dataset is too big, or you only want to display certain features in the dataset. In the example below, there are too many settlements to display labels for each settlement. Instead, it might be useful to only display the regional and national capitals. For such a use case, Rule-based Labeling is ideal.

trans-unit.source: Single Labels were selected to display the names of the settlements (red dots). A map with so much text information is unreadable and the information can hardly be understood. :::
trans-unit.target: Single Labels were selected to display the names of the settlements (red dots). A map with so much text information is unreadable and the information can hardly be understood. :::

trans-unit.source: Set up a new __Print Layout__.
trans-unit.target: Set up a new __Print Layout__.

trans-unit.target: Add a new map by clicking on the ![](/fig/30.30.2_print_layout_insert_map_icon.png) `Add map`-button.

trans-unit.source: Position the main map frame at the center of the page and size it generously so that place names, symbols, and activity markers are easy to read.
trans-unit.target: Position the main map frame at the center of the page and size it generously so that place names, symbols, and activity markers are easy to read.

trans-unit.source: Add a header using the ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_text.png) `Add Label`-tool. It should contain the following information:
trans-unit.target: Add a header using the ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_text.png) `Add Label`-tool. It should contain the following information:

trans-unit.source: The title (e.g. Sudanese Red Crescent Response)
trans-unit.target: The title (e.g. Sudanese Red Crescent Response)

trans-unit.source: The type of disaster or emergency
trans-unit.target: The type of disaster or emergency

trans-unit.source: The geographic location of the disaster
trans-unit.target: The geographic location of the disaster

trans-unit.source: The last updated date to indicate the currency of the data. Because operations evolve quickly, the date is especially important for users who need to understand how recent the information is.
trans-unit.target: The last updated date to indicate the currency of the data. Because operations evolve quickly, the date is especially important for users who need to understand how recent the information is.

trans-unit.source: Add __Organizational logos__ (e.g., International Medical Corps, Relief International, International Rescue Committee) using the ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_image.png) `Add image`-tool to ensure that each active organization can be easily identified on the map.
trans-unit.target: Add __Organizational logos__ (e.g., International Medical Corps, Relief International, International Rescue Committee) using the ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_image.png) `Add image`-tool to ensure that each active organization can be easily identified on the map.

trans-unit.source: Icons which describe the specific activity. These can also be added using the ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_image.png) `Add image`-tool. Make use of the [IFRC icons](https://learn-sims.org/style-guidance/logos-and-icons/) for map making.
trans-unit.target: Icons which describe the specific activity. These can also be added using the ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_image.png) `Add image`-tool. Make use of the [IFRC icons](https://learn-sims.org/style-guidance/logos-and-icons/) for map making.

trans-unit.source: A small overview map to help situate the disaster-affected area within a broader geographic context.
trans-unit.target: A small overview map to help situate the disaster-affected area within a broader geographic context.

trans-unit.source: __Map Composition__
trans-unit.target: __Map Composition__

trans-unit.source: A good map guides the reader in understanding the information available on the map, makes the information easily accessible, and is not overloaded with information.
trans-unit.target: A good map guides the reader in understanding the information available on the map, makes the information easily accessible, and is not overloaded with information.

trans-unit.source: In general, there are a few things to keep in mind when creating a map:
trans-unit.target: In general, there are a few things to keep in mind when creating a map:

trans-unit.source: The main map should cover the largest portion of the page and be centred.
trans-unit.target: The main map should cover the largest portion of the page and be centred.

trans-unit.source: A complete map must have:
trans-unit.target: A complete map must have:

trans-unit.source: A meaningful title
trans-unit.target: A meaningful title

trans-unit.source: All the sources used
trans-unit.target: All the sources used

trans-unit.source: A legend ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_legend.png) explaining symbols and color schemes.
trans-unit.target: A legend ![](../../fig/30.30.2_print_layout_add_legend.png) explaining symbols and color schemes.

trans-unit.source: A scale bar
trans-unit.target: A scale bar

trans-unit.source: A north arrow
trans-unit.target: A north arrow

trans-unit.source: and necessary other information to contextualize the information presented on the map.
trans-unit.target: and necessary other information to contextualize the information presented on the map.

trans-unit.source: This additional information, such as the title should be scaled accordingly.
trans-unit.target: This additional information, such as the title should be scaled accordingly.

trans-unit.source: Titles should be large so the reader can identify it as the main topic of the map.
trans-unit.target: Titles should be large so the reader can identify it as the main topic of the map.

trans-unit.source: Additional information should be smaller and moved out of the main focus of the page (e.g. at the bottom, to the sides, or in the corners).
trans-unit.target: Additional information should be smaller and moved out of the main focus of the page (e.g. at the bottom, to the sides, or in the corners).

trans-unit.source: A well-structured page layout helps the reader discern the different information on the map and makes it easier to know where to look for certain information. Frames and boxes can structure the page layout. For example, a legend can be put on the bottom or to the right of the map.
trans-unit.target: A well-structured page layout helps the reader discern the different information on the map and makes it easier to know where to look for certain information. Frames and boxes can structure the page layout. For example, a legend can be put on the bottom or to the right of the map.

trans-unit.source: Areas of operation for each organization
trans-unit.target: Areas of operation for each organization

trans-unit.source: Place the logos of each organization within the districts where they are active.
trans-unit.target: Place the logos of each organization within the districts where they are active.

trans-unit.source: Add activity icons to indicate the specific types of work each organization is carrying out in those districts.
trans-unit.target: Add activity icons to indicate the specific types of work each organization is carrying out in those districts.

trans-unit.source: To illustrate the link between organization logos, activity icons, and their corresponding areas, arrows can be added to the map. Using this feature helps clearly emphasize the relationships and improves overall readability.
trans-unit.target: To illustrate the link between organization logos, activity icons, and their corresponding areas, arrows can be added to the map. Using this feature helps clearly emphasize the relationships and improves overall readability.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} How to add arrow item The arrows ![](../../fig/mActionAddArrow.png) can be added from the left-side panel in the Print Layout. Click and drag on the layout to draw it, then use the Item Properties panel to adjust the arrow’s style, thickness, color, and direction. Arrows can be moved or resized like any other layout item, allowing you to visually connect map features, icons, or labels. :::
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} How to add arrow item The arrows ![](../../fig/mActionAddArrow.png) can be added from the left-side panel in the Print Layout. Click and drag on the layout to draw it, then use the Item Properties panel to adjust the arrow’s style, thickness, color, and direction. Arrows can be moved or resized like any other layout item, allowing you to visually connect map features, icons, or labels. :::

trans-unit.source: Epidemological overview map
trans-unit.target: Epidemological overview map

trans-unit.source: Introduction
trans-unit.target: Introduction

trans-unit.source: Epidemiological overview maps are among the most frequently requested analytical products during the early stages of an outbreak response. These maps synthesize when, where, and how intensely disease transmission and mortality are occurring across affected areas. By visualizing weekly case incidence and identifying whether transmission is increasing, stable, or decreasing in each location, responders gain rapid insight into the evolving dynamics of the epidemic. A related view, incorporating weekly deaths and mortality rates, supports assessment of disease severity and potential gaps in access to care. These outputs are routinely included in Situation Reports, operational updates, and epidemiological briefings, and serve as key reference tools during coordination meetings. In this section, we will explore the essential components of epidemiological overview maps and demonstrate how to produce them in QGIS using simulated weekly case and mortality data.
trans-unit.target: Epidemiological overview maps are among the most frequently requested analytical products during the early stages of an outbreak response. These maps synthesize when, where, and how intensely disease transmission and mortality are occurring across affected areas. By visualizing weekly case incidence and identifying whether transmission is increasing, stable, or decreasing in each location, responders gain rapid insight into the evolving dynamics of the epidemic. A related view, incorporating weekly deaths and mortality rates, supports assessment of disease severity and potential gaps in access to care. These outputs are routinely included in Situation Reports, operational updates, and epidemiological briefings, and serve as key reference tools during coordination meetings. In this section, we will explore the essential components of epidemiological overview maps and demonstrate how to produce them in QGIS using simulated weekly case and mortality data.

trans-unit.source: In this tutorial, you will learn how to create a multivariable map to generate an epidemiological overview of a cholera outbreak in Somalia. Using a fictional dataset with weekly infection counts and death rates, you will visualize the situation for a selected week by applying several QGIS visualization tools.
trans-unit.target: In this tutorial, you will learn how to create a multivariable map to generate an epidemiological overview of a cholera outbreak in Somalia. Using a fictional dataset with weekly infection counts and death rates, you will visualize the situation for a selected week by applying several QGIS visualization tools.

trans-unit.source: About the Dataset
trans-unit.target: About the Dataset

trans-unit.source: The dataset contains epidemiological parameters for Somalia at the regional level, covering a 12-week period during a cholera outbreak. The parameters include weekly new cases, weekly cumulative cases, and the weekly death rate. Download the dataset [here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/module_4/tutorial_epi_map/Som_outbreak_epi.gpkg).
trans-unit.target: The dataset contains epidemiological parameters for Somalia at the regional level, covering a 12-week period during a cholera outbreak. The parameters include weekly new cases, weekly cumulative cases, and the weekly death rate. Download the dataset [here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/module_4/tutorial_epi_map/Som_outbreak_epi.gpkg).

trans-unit.target: Fields used in this tutorial

trans-unit.target: **Field**

trans-unit.target: **Contents**

trans-unit.source: **weekX_inf_new**
trans-unit.target: **weekX_inf_new**

trans-unit.target: Count of new weekly infections in week X of the outbreak

trans-unit.source: **weekX_inf_cul**
trans-unit.target: **weekX_inf_cul**

trans-unit.source: Cumulative count of infections in week X
trans-unit.target: Cumulative count of infections in week X

trans-unit.target: **weekX_dc**
